content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1126 R Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

December 23, 1975

Samuel V. McGrath
City Attorney
City of Richmond
Richmond, CA 94804

Dear Mr. McGrath:

     The Commission is in receipt of your letter of December 2, 1975, written on behalf of the Bay Area City Attorneys Association and urging removal of the city attorneys from the roles of criminal and civil prosecutors under the Political Reform Act.

     The Commission is aware of the difficult ethical problems faced by city attorneys as a result of the potentially conflicting obligations imposed by the Political Reform Act and by the charters pursuant to which they are appointed or elected, and will be seeking corrective legislation to remedy this problem in the near future.

     If you have any suggestions relative to the type of legislation that would be best from your perspective, I encourage you to forward them to our staff. Thank you for your interest in this matter. If you have any questions, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

DHL:plh

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

CITY OF RICHMOND
CALIFORNIA 94804

SAMUEL V. McGRATH
CITY ATTORNEY

(415) 232-1212, EXTENSION 351

December 2, 1975

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein
Chairman Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

     The Political Reform Act of 1974 provides, through Government
Code Section 91001, that:

     (1) The city attorneys and district attorneys of this State
have concurrent powers and responsibilities with the Attorney
General for enforcing the criminal provisions of the Political
Reform Act of 1974.

     (2) Each city attorney is the civil prosecutor with respect
to his city and its agencies.

     As its president, the Bay Area City Attorneys Association has
directed me to inform you that, essentially for the reasons stated
below, the Association strongly urges the removal of the city
attorneys from the roles of criminal and civil prosecutors under
that Act.

     Most of the city attorneys in this State are appointed by
their city councils. Thus, §91001 places such city attorneys in
a position where, when councilmen violate the Act, the attorneys
have obligations to prosecute the very individuals who have the
power to fire them. The Association emphatically asserts that the
imposition of such a conflict of interests situation upon the city
attorneys is wholly inequitable and unjustified and should be
remedied by the Legislature as rapidly as possible.

     In the case of the county counsels, who are in a like position
with regard to their Boards of Supervisors, the act judicially re-
frained from placing them in such a conflict of interests situation
by having an elected official, the local district attorney, assume
the civil and criminal prosecutorial roles with regard to county
matters. Our Association feels that the city attorneys should be
equally exempt by having the Act amended to place those roles with
the Attorney General and/or the local district attorney.

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein
December 2, 1975
Page Two


     The Association plans to pursue this matter with the State
Legislature, League of California Cities, and Fair Political
Practices Commission. Any assistance which you can provide
would be most appreciated.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Samuel V. McGrath, President
                                        Bay Area City Attorneys Association

SVMcG:JK

sections.question_synthetic: Should city attorneys be removed from their roles as criminal and civil prosecutors under the Political Reform Act of 1974 due to conflicts of interest when they must prosecute council members who have power to fire them?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Commission acknowledges the difficult ethical problems faced by city attorneys due to potentially conflicting obligations under the Political Reform Act and municipal charters, and will seek corrective legislation to remedy the problem in the near future.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is correspondence between the FPPC and city attorneys, not a traditional advice letter. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. The FPPC response is relatively brief and non-committal. Document quality and OCR accuracy issues present some legibility challenges but key content is extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should city attorneys be removed from their roles as criminal and civil prosecutors under the Political Reform Act of 1974 due to conflicts of interest when they must prosecute council members who have power to fire them?

CONCLUSION: The Commission acknowledges the difficult ethical problems faced by city attorneys due to potentially conflicting obligations under the Political Reform Act and municipal charters, and will seek corrective legislation to remedy the problem in the near future.

embedding.summary: The Bay Area City Attorneys Association, through City Attorney Samuel McGrath, requests that the FPPC support removing city attorneys from prosecutorial roles under the Political Reform Act due to conflicts of interest. The FPPC Chairman Daniel Lowenstein responds by acknowledging the ethical problems and indicating the Commission will seek corrective legislation.
